request.question: What nationality is the only athlete who has won both Senior All-Ireland Championship medal and this contest in 2005 in Melbourne attended by 91,898 spectators.

title: Tadhg Kennelly
Kerry GAA honours
Emer Dillon
David Curtin
Rachel Moloney
Mark Davoren
Ireland National League (rugby league)
2005 AFL Grand Final
Jenny Duffy
Brian Farrell (Gaelic footballer)
sentences: Tadhg Kennelly (born 1 July 1981) is an Irish sportsperson known for his top level careers in both Gaelic football and Australian rules football.
He is the only holder of both an AFL Premiership medallion and a Senior All-Ireland Championship medal, the highest possible achievement in both sports.
He has also represented Ireland in the International Rules Series.
Kerry are by far the most successful team in the history of Gaelic football.
They have won 37 All-Ireland Senior Football Championship and have appeared in countless other All-Ireland Football Finals.
Kerry footballers have been garlanded with countless awards and hold numerous individual records in the sport.

Pat Spillane received nine All Stars during a glittering career, a feat matched by no other Gaelic footballer, while Tadhg Kennelly is the only holder of both an AFL Premiership medallion and a Senior All-Ireland Championship medal, the highest possible achievement in the sports of Australian rules football and Gaelic football.
Here are Kerry's honours.
Emer Dillon born 1984 in Cork is a camogie player and a marketing executive, winner of All Ireland camogie medals in 2002, 2005, 2006, and 2009 and a camogie All Star in 2005.
Emer was selected as player of the match in the 2005 All-Ireland Senior final.
She is also a prominent hockey player and has represented both Munster and Ireland.
She holds a Senior county championship medal with Carrigdhoun and also Junior and under-age championship honours with Ballygarvan.
She has won All-Ireland Senior, Intermediate and Minor medals as well as inter-provincial honours but did not participate in the 2008 championship due to study.
David Curtin (born 13 September 1980) is an inter-county hurling player who plays for Dublin.
He plays his club hurling for Dublin side Ballyboden St. Enda's.
In 2005, he will be remembered for his astounding performance against Laois which confirmed Dublins All-Ireland status for the 2006 championship.
Curtin finished the game with an impressive scoreline of 1-7.
In the 2006 All-Ireland championship, this feat was repeated when Dublin swept aside Westmeath to once retain their Liam McCarthy Cup status.
He finished the 2006 championship with a scoreline of 0-33, a scoreline which left him 5th of the top scorer list despite Dublins lack of progress in the All-Ireland championship.
Curtin was the Dublin hurling captain for 2005.
David was named on the 2006 Dublin Bus/Evening Herald Blue Star hurling XV at Centre Forward.
Curtin won his first Dublin Senior Hurling Championship medal with his club Ballyboden St Endas in October 2007.
Since then Boden have won 4 titles in a row.
The 2007 Dublin championship win qualified Ballyboden to play in their first ever Leinster Senior Club Hurling Championship game against Oulart the Ballagh GAA of Wexford.
Boden won the game by 1-17 to 0-15 with Curtin scoring a total of 0-04.
Rachel Moloney born 1982 in Cork is a camogie player and a marketing executive, winner of All Ireland camogie medals in 2008, and 2009 and camogie All Star awards in 2005, 2008 and 2009.
She holds Ashbourne Cup medals with U.C.C. as well as Minor, Junior, Intermediate and Senior All-Ireland championship honours with Cork.
Has won inter-provincial honours with Munster and is the side's free-taker and top scorer.
Rachel is coach to her club's Under-14 team.
Mark Davoren is an Irish Gaelic footballer for Dublin and Kilmacud Crokes.
He made his debut for Dublin against Kerry in the National Football League (Ireland) in 2009 scoring a total of 1-03 in the game which finished as a draw in Parnell Park.
He won an All-Ireland Senior Club Football Championship medal with Kilmacud Crokes on St Patricks Day 2009 in Croke Park against Crosmaglen Rangers.
He scored a goal in the game.
Davoren won a Leinster Senior Club Football Championship medal with Crokes against Rhode of Offalyin 2008 at Parnell Park, he scored 1-00 in the final.
He won the Dublin Senior Football Championship in 2008 by beating St Oliver Plunketts/Eoghan Ruadh in Parnell Park scoring a goal in the process.
He won an AFL Dublin Division One title with Kilmacud Crokes in 2008, although he did not make an appearance in the league final.
The All-Ireland Rugby League Championship (now the McGettigans All-Ireland League for sponsorship reasons) was first played for in 1997 under the title All-Ireland Challenge Cup.
Since then it has also been known as the All-Ireland League, Elite League and the All-Ireland Championship.
The 2005 AFL Grand Final was an Australian rules football game contested between the Sydney Swans and West Coast Eagles at the Melbourne Cricket Ground on 24 September 2005.
It was the 109th annual Grand Final of the Australian Football League (formerly the Victorian Football League), staged to determine the premiers for the 2005 AFL season.
The match, attended by 91,898 spectators, was won by Sydney by a margin of four points, marking the club's fourth Premiership and their first since 1933.
Jennifer "Jenny" Duffy (born 1983 in Cork) is a camogie player, footballer and camogie development officer, winner of All Ireland camogie medals in 2009.
A former student at Cork IT, Jenny is the Camogie Regional Development officer in South Leinster.
She is the holder of All-Ireland championship and league medals with CIT as well as Senior and Junior All-Ireland championship medals and National League.
She won a Senior county championship with her club and captained CIT to the Ashbourne Shield title in 2006.
Brian Farrell is a Gaelic footballer from Nobber, County Meath, Ireland.
He plays with the Meath county team and his local club Nobber.

He first came to national attention when he was part of the Meath team that made it to the 2002 All-Ireland Minor Football Championship final but lost out to Derry having already been beaten by Longford in the Leinster Championship final.
The following year, he was part of the Meath side that won the Leinster and All-Ireland Junior Football Championships.
At club level, he has won County and Leinster Junior medals in 2002 and an All-Ireland Junior Club Football Championship medal in 2003.
In 2010, Farrell won a Meath Intermediate Football Championship medal.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: What nationality is the only athlete who has won both Senior All-Ireland Championship medal and this contest in 2005 in Melbourne attended by 91,898 spectators.
hop1_docs: 2005 AFL Grand Final | The 2005 AFL Grand Final was an Australian rules football game contested between the Sydney Swans and West Coast Eagles at the Melbourne Cricket Ground on 24 September 2005.  It was the 109th annual Grand Final of the Australian Football League (formerly the Victorian Football League), staged to determine the premiers for the 2005 AFL season.  The match, attended by 91,898 spectators, was won by Sydney by a margin of four points, marking the club's fourth Premiership and their first since 1933.

Emer Dillon | Emer Dillon born 1984 in Cork is a camogie player and a marketing executive, winner of All Ireland camogie medals in 2002, 2005, 2006, and 2009 and a camogie All Star in 2005.  Emer was selected as player of the match in the 2005 All-Ireland Senior final.  She is also a prominent hockey player and has represented both Munster and Ireland.  She holds a Senior county championship medal with Carrigdhoun and also Junior and under-age championship honours with Ballygarvan.  She has won All-Ireland Senior, Intermediate and Minor medals as well as inter-provincial honours but did not participate in the 2008 championship due to study.

Cookstown Fr. Rock's GAC | Cookstown Father Rocks Gaelic Athletic Club is a Gaelic Athletic Association club based in Cookstown in County Tyrone, Northern Ireland.  In recent years Owen Mulligan and Raymond Mulgrew have both won All-Ireland Senior Football Championship medals with the Tyrone senior team.  The club also had five players who won All-Ireland Minor Football Championship medals in the same day in 2004.

2005 All-Ireland Senior Camogie Championship | The 2005 All-Ireland Senior Camogie Championship—known as the Foras na Gaeilge All-Ireland Senior Camogie Championship for sponsorship reasons—was the high point of the 2005 season in the sport of camogie.  The championship was won for the 21st time by Cork who defeated Tipperary by a four-point margin in the final and became part of the legendary “rebel treble” of 2005 when Cork won the senior hurling, camogie and ladies’ football titles.  The attendance was 14,350.

Ollie Murphy | Ollie Murphy is a Meath Footballer.  He plays club football for Carnaross.  He came to national prominence in 1999 when he was one of Meath's best player's as they won the All-Ireland Senior Football Championship that year, scoring the decisive goal in that year's All Ireland final.  This was his second All Ireland senior medal as he was a substitute when Meath won the 1996 All-Ireland Senior Football Championship.  He is also the holder of 3 Leinster Senior Football Championship medals.  Ollie has won 2 GAA All Stars Awards.  Murphys best season was the 2001 championship where he put in a number of superb display's as Meath reached another All Ireland final.  In that final he had to leave the field of play with a hand injury.  In recent years his form has been not up to what it once was and he has failed to warrant a starting position.  Ollie made his senior inter-county debut playing for London in the 1994 Connacht Championship against Galway.  It was his one and only appearance for the Exiles.  Murphy's Benny father was also an inter-county player, he lined out for his native Fermanagh

Aoife Murray | Aoife Murray (born 1984 in Dunmanway) is a Camogie player and quantity surveyor based in Cork, winner of All Ireland medals in 2002, 2005, 2006, 2008, 2009, 2014 and 2015.  All Star awards in 2004, 2008, 2009, 2012, 2014 and 2015.  Minor, Junior, Intermediate as well as her Senior All-Ireland medals and Munster championship honours in all grades.  Won three county Senior championship medals with Cloughduv and was captain in 2005 for their third county title.  Sister of former Cork Senior hurler, Kevin Murray, who won an All- Ireland medal in 1999.  A series of impressive saves in the 2009 All Ireland final won for her the "player of the match” award from RTÉ.

1989 All-Ireland Senior Camogie Championship | The 1989 All-Ireland Senior Camogie Championship was won by Killkenny who defeated Cork by an eight-point margin in the final.  The match drew an attendance of 3,024 and marked Angela Downey’s ninth All Ireland medal.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - 2005 AFL Grand Final (passage 1): "match, attended by 91,898 spectators" — establishes the contest.
  - Emer Dillon (passage 2): "winner of All Ireland camogie medals in 2002, 2005, 2006" (no AFL link).
  - Aoife Murray (passage 6): "winner of All Ireland medals in 2002, 2005, 2006..." (no AFL link).
  - Cookstown Fr. Rock's GAC (passage 3): mentions Owen Mulligan and Raymond Mulgrew "have both won All‑Ireland Senior Football Championship medals" (no AFL link).
  - Ollie Murphy (passage 5): "won the All‑Ireland Senior Football Championship" (no AFL link).
  - Candidate Tadhg Kennelly: not present in supplied passages (no mention in passages).

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - No supplied passage connects any named All‑Ireland Senior medal holder to winning the 2005 AFL Grand Final.
  - The question refers to "the only athlete who has won both" — that specific individual is not identified in the passages.

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - A source that explicitly names the athlete who has won both a Senior All‑Ireland Championship medal and the 2005 AFL Grand Final.
  - The nationality of that athlete (as stated on a reliable page).

- retrieval_direction (priority order):
  1. "Tadhg Kennelly" (page / article about Tadhg Kennelly) — check for both an All‑Ireland Senior medal and 2005 AFL premiership, and nationality.
  2. "List of players in the 2005 Sydney Swans premiership team" or "2005 Sydney Swans team list" — to find which players won the Grand Final.
  3. "players who have won both an All‑Ireland Senior Football Championship and an AFL premiership" — summary/list pages.
  4. "Tadhg Kennelly All‑Ireland 1997 Kerry" (if needed to confirm the All‑Ireland medal year).
response.hop2_query: Tadhg Kennelly nationality All-Ireland Senior Football Championship 2005 AFL Grand Final
hop2_docs: Tadhg Kennelly | Tadhg Kennelly (born 1 July 1981) is an Irish sportsperson known for his top level careers in both Gaelic football and Australian rules football.  He is the only holder of both an AFL Premiership medallion and a Senior All-Ireland Championship medal, the highest possible achievement in both sports.  He has also represented Ireland in the International Rules Series.

Kerry GAA honours | Kerry are by far the most successful team in the history of Gaelic football.  They have won 37 All-Ireland Senior Football Championship and have appeared in countless other All-Ireland Football Finals.  Kerry footballers have been garlanded with countless awards and hold numerous individual records in the sport.  Pat Spillane received nine All Stars during a glittering career, a feat matched by no other Gaelic footballer, while Tadhg Kennelly is the only holder of both an AFL Premiership medallion and a Senior All-Ireland Championship medal, the highest possible achievement in the sports of Australian rules football and Gaelic football.  Here are Kerry's honours.

Noel Kennelly | Noel Kennelly is a footballer from the Listowel Emmets club in North Kerry.  He is a son of former Kerry great Tim Kennelly and older brother of Tadhg Kennelly.  He won an All Ireland Medal with Kerry in 2000.  He won an All Ireland Under 21 medal in 1998 and played in the losing final of 1999, he also played in the losing All Ireland Minor final in 1996.
2005 All-Ireland Senior Football Championship Final | 2005 All-Ireland Senior Football Championship Final
2005 All-Ireland Senior Ladies' Football Championship Final | 2005 All-Ireland Senior Ladies' Football Championship Final

2005 All-Ireland Senior Football Championship | The All-Ireland Senior Football Championship 2005, known for sponsorship reasons as the 2005 Bank of Ireland All-Ireland Senior Football Championship was the premier Gaelic football competition in 2005.  It consisted of 33 teams and began on Sunday 7 May 2005.  Few surprises came during the championship with the dominance of the Ulster teams evident once again.  Gaelic football's "Big Three" of this era - Armagh, Kerry, Tyrone - all progressed to the semi-finals.

James Nallen | James Nallen (born 1973 in Castlebar) is an All Star winning Gaelic footballer for Mayo.  He played in five All-Ireland finals, including an All-Ireland final replay against Meath in 1996, and won two All Stars, in 1996 and 2004.  He won a National Football League medal in 2001.  However, he never won an All-Ireland medal.  He retired from inter-county football in 2010.  At club level he had much success with Crossmolina winning Mayo Senior Football Championships in 1995, 1999, 2000, 2002, 2005, 2006 Connacht Senior Club Football Championships in 1999, 2000, 2002 and an All-Ireland Senior Club Football Championship in 2001.
hop2_titles: 2005 All-Ireland Senior Ladies' Football Championship Final
response.summary_2: Irish. Tadhg Kennelly is described in the passages as an Irish sportsperson and the only holder of both an AFL Premiership medallion and a Senior All‑Ireland Championship medal.
